perturbation.original_text: All disputes or claims hereunder shall be resolved by arbitration in McLean, Virginia, pursuant to the rules of the American Arbitration Association.
perturbation.changed_text: All disputes or claims hereunder shall be resolved at the discretion of Network 1.
perturbation.explanation: By giving Network 1 complete discretion over dispute resolution, the contract effectively eliminates the consumer's right to a fair and neutral arbitration process, potentially violating consumer protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal law dictates standards for arbitration clauses and processes, ensuring fairness and neutrality. Granting one party complete discretion over dispute resolution circumvents these standards and can render the arbitration agreement unenforceable. The FAA favors arbitration, but not when the terms are unconscionable or fundamentally unfair.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a structured arbitration process with complete discretion granted to Network 1, effectively eliminating the consumer's right to a fair and neutral dispute resolution. This contradicts the principle of fair arbitration, as suggested by the FAA. Scraped Law Snippets 1 and 2, both from 9 U.S. Code § 2, state the validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate, suggesting a legal standard for arbitration agreements. By giving one party full discretion, the changed text undermines the intended arbitration process, potentially making the agreement unenforceable due to unfairness.

perturbation.original_text: You shall be responsible for and pay all expenses and fees incurred by you, including but not limited to your business overhead, transportation, state and federal income taxes, self-employment tax, unemployment tax and workers' compensation.
perturbation.changed_text: You shall be responsible for and pay all expenses and fees incurred by you, including but not limited to your business overhead, transportation, state and federal income taxes, self-employment tax.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'unemployment tax and workers' compensation' could be seen as an attempt to misclassify employees as independent contractors, potentially violating labor laws related to worker classification and benefits.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal tax law (26 U.S. Code § 3508) and corresponding state laws define the criteria for independent contractor classification. Employers are not required to pay unemployment tax or provide workers' compensation for properly classified independent contractors. However, misclassifying an employee as an independent contractor to avoid these expenses is illegal. Omitting the responsibility for unemployment tax and workers' compensation payments in the contract raises suspicion of potential misclassification, especially if the actual working relationship resembles employment.

Treatment of real estate agents and direct sellers (a) General rule For purposes of this title, in the case of services performed as a qualified real estate agent or as a direct seller- (1) the individual performing such services shall not be treated as an employee, and (2) the person for whom such services are performed shall not be treated
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 3508 - Treatment of real estate agents and direct­ sellers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 3508 - Treatment of real estate agents and direct­ sellers U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) General rule For purposes of this title, in the case of services performed as a qualified real estate agent or as a direct seller— (1) the individual performing such services shall not be treated as an employee, and (2) the person for whom such services are performed shall not be treated as an employer. (b) Definitions For purposes of this section— (1) Qualified real estate agent The term “ qualified real estate agent ” means any individual who is a sales person if— (A) such individual is a licensed real estate agent, (B) substantially all of the remuneration (whether or not paid in cash) for the services performed by such individual as a real estate agent is directly related to sales or other output (including the performance of services) rather than to the number of hours worked, and (C) the services performed by the individual are performed pursuant to a written contract between such individual and the person for whom the services are performed and such contract provides that the individual will not be treated as an employee with respect to such services for Federal tax purposes. (2) Direct seller The term “ direct seller ” means any person if— (A) such person— (i) is engaged in the trade or business of selling (or soliciting the sale of) consumer products to any buyer on a buy-sell basis, a deposit-commission basis, or any similar basis which the Secretary prescribes by regulations, for resale (by the buyer or any other person) in the home or otherwise than in a permanent retail establishment, (ii) is engaged in the trade or business of selling (or soliciting the sale of) consumer products in the home or otherwise than in a permanent retail establishment, or
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'unemployment tax and workers' compensation' from the changed text, as compared to the original, directly contradicts the implied obligation to properly classify employees. Although the provided law snippets (26 U.S. Code § 3508) focus on the classification of real estate agents and direct sellers as independent contractors, the Law Explanation argues that omitting these responsibilities raises suspicion of misclassification, particularly if the work resembles employment. By removing these specific obligations, the changed text weakens the protection afforded to employees, as it suggests an attempt to avoid employer responsibilities like unemployment tax and workers' compensation. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: The Affiliate's Fees outlined in Exhibit A attached hereto and incorporated herein shall be subject to modification by Network 1, at its discretion, upon the giving of thirty-days (30) prior written notification.
perturbation.changed_text: The Affiliate's Fees outlined in Exhibit A attached hereto and incorporated herein shall be subject to modification by Network 1, at its discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement of 'thirty-days (30) prior written notification' grants Network 1 the ability to change Affiliate fees at any time without prior notice, which creates a legal risk by violating principles of contract law requiring good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract. Removing the 30-day notice provision allows Network 1 to unilaterally alter the fees, potentially depriving the Affiliate of the benefit of their bargain without warning. While the contract doesn't explicitly involve the sale of goods, the principle of good faith and fair dealing is widely applicable to contracts in general and removing the notice provision raises concerns about potential bad faith conduct.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The omission of the 'thirty-days (30) prior written notification' directly contradicts the implied obligation of good faith, as highlighted in UCC § 1-304, provided in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By removing the notification period, Network 1 can arbitrarily alter fees, potentially violating the good faith principle. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both emphasize the resulting potential deprivation of the Affiliate's benefit without warning, which signifies a legal contradiction arising from the omission.
